title: Asymptotic distinguishability of Haar-averaged measurement models
authors: Ludmiła Marcinkowska, Łukasz Pawela, Marcin Markiewicz, Zbigniew Puchała
abstract: We study discrimination problems generated by the same basic Haar-random measurement mechanism at two observational levels. First, we derive an explicit expression for the type-II error in the task of discriminating a Haar-random measure-and-prepare channel from the identity channel $\mathbb{I}$, using a coherence-sensitive entangled tester. Second, after passing to the induced classical measurement records, we compare two random measurement models: one induced by a single collective unitary of the form $U^{\otimes (n_1+n_2)}$ with $U\in U(d)$, and another induced by independent local unitaries $U_1^{\otimes n_1}\otimes U_2^{\otimes n_2}$. For the associated Haar-averaged aggregate histogram laws, in which the block of origin of each count is not retained, we obtain closed-form formulas and quantify their discrepancy through the total variation distance. We derive asymptotic expressions in the fixed-$N$, large-$d$ regime, the fixed-$d$, large-$N$ regime, the sparse joint-scaling regime $N=o(\sqrt d)$, and the critical scaling regime $N/\sqrt d\to c$. We also identify the block-resolved pair-of-histograms law, showing that the aggregate total variation distance is a coarse-grained lower bound on the distinguishability available when block labels are retained.
summary: This work rigorously analyzes how distinguishable two measurement models are when the underlying randomness is either collective (shared unitary) or local (independent unitaries). By calculating the Total Variation Distance in various asymptotic limits, the authors show that the level of observation—whether aggregate or block-resolved—fundamentally changes the measurable distinguishability.
main_problem: The paper studies the asymptotic distinguishability between measurement models generated by a single collective unitary versus those generated by independent local unitaries.
main_result: The distinguishability, quantified by the Total Variation Distance (TVD), depends critically on the observation level: the aggregate statistics yield a nontrivial limit, while the block-resolved statistics show perfect distinguishability in certain limits.
method: The authors use Weingarten calculus and combinatorial analysis to derive closed-form expressions and asymptotic limits for the TVD in various scaling regimes.
model_or_system: The model involves Haar-random measurement-and-prepare channels acting on a composite system of $N=n_1+n_2$ subsystems of local dimension $d$. The comparison is between the collective channel $Q(N)_U$ and the block-separated channel $Q(n_1, n_2)_{U,V}$.
key_observables: Type-II error probability, Total Variation Distance (TVD) between aggregate and block-resolved histogram laws, and the block-resolved pair-of-histograms law.
important_parameters: The analysis covers multiple asymptotic regimes: fixed-$N$, large-$d$; fixed-$d$, large-$N$; sparse joint-scaling ($N = o(\sqrt{d})$); and critical scaling ($N/\sqrt{d} 	o c$).
main_assumptions: The analysis distinguishes between the aggregate (block-unresolved) and block-resolved statistics, and relies on asymptotic expansions derived from Haar measure integration.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the discrimination protocol using entangled testers and show the relationship between the collective and multipartite measurement models.
paper_structure: The paper first addresses channel discrimination (Type-II error) and then focuses on comparing the aggregate vs. block-resolved histogram laws using the TVD, deriving specific asymptotic limits for different scaling regimes.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical tools developed for quantifying distinguishability between structured vs. unstructured quantum processes (shared vs. independent unitaries) are highly relevant for understanding resource quantification in quantum information theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Discrimination protocol between the identity channel I and the Haar-random measurement channel Q(N) U . The maximally entangled state |ψ⟩is prepared across N subsystems. The channel Q(N) U acts collectively on these subsystems, followed by the joint measurement {Π0, Π1}.

Figure 2: Equivalent representations of the collective model. The multipartite implementation using N copies of Q(1) U is equivalent to the collective channel Q(N) U acting on the product input state |0⟩⊗N, producing the classical outcome vector k = (k0, . . . , kN−1).

Figure 3: Equivalent representations of the block model. The multipartite implementation using n1 copies of Q(1) U and n2 copies of Q(1) V is equivalent to two collective channels Q(n1) U and Q(n2) V acting independently on the corresponding subsystems, producing the classical outcome vectors k(1) and k(2). In the aggregate experiment, the observer retains only the total histogram obtained after forgetting the block assignment of the outcomes.
Figure 4: Limiting aggregate total variation distance between P (N)(λ) and P (n1,n2)(λ), i.e. TVD∞(3, α), as a function of α.
Figure 5: Limiting aggregate total variation distance between P (N)(λ) and P (n1,n2)(λ), i.e. TVD∞(4, α), as a function of α.
